course_info.title: User Research Methods
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

learning_objectives: Develop a research plan that aligns methods with research questions
Write research questions that are specific, answerable, and actionable
Select appropriate methods based on project constraints and goals
readings: Just Enough Research by Erika Hall, Ch. 3-4
Observing the User Experience by Goodman, Kuniavsky & Moed, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: User research is the discipline of learning about users through observation and measurement. This course builds fluency in the full range of research methods a UX professional needs.
key_concepts: Research questions vs. interview questions vs. survey questions
The research question hierarchy: strategic, tactical, operational
Method selection matrix: attitudinal vs. behavioral, qualitative vs. quantitative
Research timelines and resource planning
Stakeholder alignment: getting buy-in for research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a real research plan from a product team, analyzing how research questions drove method selection

review.value: A specific question that guides the study design — 'How do first-time users discover the search feature?' not 'Is the search good?'
Framework plotting methods on two axes: attitudinal (what people say) vs. behavioral (what people do), and qualitative (why) vs. quantitative (how many)

learning_objectives: Conduct contextual inquiry sessions in users' real environments
Apply the master-apprentice model of contextual observation
Capture rich observational data including environment, behavior, and artifacts
readings: Contextual Design by Hugh Beyer & Karen Holtzblatt, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Contextual inquiry goes beyond interviews by observing users in their actual environment while they perform real tasks. It reveals the full context that shapes behavior — the workspace, tools, interruptions, and workarounds.
key_concepts: The master-apprentice model: user is the expert, researcher learns
Four principles: context, partnership, interpretation, focus
Capturing observations: behavior, environment, artifacts, quotes
Affinity diagramming contextual data
Contextual inquiry vs. ethnography vs. observation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Video analysis of a contextual inquiry session, identifying effective and ineffective researcher behaviors
setup_requirements: Video recording equipment for practice sessions

review.value: The researcher adopts the role of apprentice learning from the user-expert, asking 'show me how you do that' rather than 'tell me what you do'
Context (observe in real environment), Partnership (collaborate with user), Interpretation (share your understanding for validation), Focus (direct attention to relevant activities)

learning_objectives: Design surveys with valid and reliable question structures
Avoid common survey biases: leading questions, double-barreled questions, response bias
Analyze survey data to extract meaningful quantitative insights
readings: Survey Methodology by Groves et al., Ch. 7-9
Measuring the User Experience by Tullis & Albert, Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: Surveys are the most common quantitative research method in UX. When designed well, they provide statistically significant data at scale. When designed poorly, they produce misleading numbers.
key_concepts: Question types: Likert scales, semantic differential, ranking, open-ended
Question order effects and how to mitigate them
Sample size determination and statistical significance
Common biases: acquiescence bias, social desirability, central tendency
Standardized instruments: SUS, CSAT, NPS — when and how to use them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a badly written survey together — identifying and fixing 10 common survey design errors
setup_requirements: Survey platform access (Google Forms or Typeform)
Statistical calculator for sample size estimation

review.value: Standardized 10-item questionnaire providing a single usability score (0-100). Industry average is 68. Quick, reliable, and widely benchmarked.
A question that asks about two things at once — 'Is the app fast and easy to use?' — making the response uninterpretable. Always split into separate questions.

learning_objectives: Design and execute a diary study to capture longitudinal user behavior
Choose appropriate prompting strategies to maintain participant engagement
Analyze diary study data for patterns over time
readings: Diary Studies: Understanding Long-Term Behavior (NNg article)
Observing the User Experience by Goodman, Kuniavsky & Moed, Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Some user behaviors only emerge over time. Diary studies capture real experiences in context over days, weeks, or months — revealing patterns that a single interview session never could.
key_concepts: Diary study formats: text, photo, video, structured prompts
Snippet vs. elicitation diary methods
Participant recruitment and retention strategies
Prompting schedules: event-contingent, interval-contingent, signal-contingent
Analyzing temporal patterns and experience curves
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a 5-day micro diary study using a mobile survey tool, reviewing sample entries, and identifying emerging patterns
setup_requirements: Mobile diary study platform (dscout or Google Forms)

review.value: Participants log entries whenever a specific event occurs (e.g., 'every time you use public transit') — captures real experiences in context but relies on participant memory and motivation
The trajectory of user experience over time — how initial frustration, learning, and eventual mastery or abandonment unfold over repeated use

learning_objectives: Conduct open and closed card sorting studies to inform information architecture
Run tree tests to validate navigation structures
Analyze card sorting dendrograms and similarity matrices
readings: Information Architecture by Rosenfeld, Morville & Arango, Ch. 11
Card Sorting: Designing Usable Categories by Donna Spencer, Ch. 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Card sorting and tree testing are the foundational methods for building and validating information architecture. They reveal how users mentally organize information — often very differently from how organizations structure it internally.
key_concepts: Open card sorting: participants create their own categories
Closed card sorting: participants sort into predefined categories
Hybrid card sorting: predefined categories with option to create new ones
Tree testing: validating findability without visual design influence
Analysis: similarity matrices, dendrograms, task success rates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a live open card sort using OptimalSort with the class as participants, then analyzing the results in real time
setup_requirements: OptimalSort or similar card sorting tool
Prepared card set for demonstration (30-40 content items)

review.value: A tree diagram showing how participants clustered cards — items grouped by more participants appear closer together, revealing natural category boundaries
Testing whether users can find items in a text-only navigation hierarchy — isolates findability from visual design, measuring pure information architecture effectiveness

learning_objectives: Synthesize mixed-method research findings into coherent narratives
Create research deliverables that drive design decisions
Present findings to stakeholders with appropriate framing and recommendations
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Ch. 1-3
Presenting Design Work by Donna Spencer, Ch. on Research Presentations

lecture_notes.introduction: Research that sits in a report nobody reads has zero impact. The final step of every research project is translating findings into a compelling story that drives action.
key_concepts: The research narrative arc: context, findings, implications, recommendations
Atomic research: tagging and storing reusable research nuggets
Data visualization: choosing the right chart for the data type
Tailoring findings for different audiences: designers, developers, executives
Research democratization: making findings accessible to the whole team
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming a raw research data set into a 10-minute stakeholder presentation — from sticky notes to slides
setup_requirements: Presentation software (Keynote, Google Slides, or Figma)
Sample research data set for exercise

review.value: Breaking research findings into small, tagged, reusable units (experiments, facts, insights, recommendations) stored in a searchable repository — prevents re-researching the same questions
Context (what we studied and why) > Findings (what we discovered, with evidence) > Implications (what it means for the product) > Recommendations (what to do next)

questions.question: A product manager asks you to 'find out if users like the new dashboard.' Rewrite this as a well-formed research question, then recommend an appropriate research method and explain why.
questions.answer: Rewritten research question: 'How effectively does the new dashboard help users identify and act on their key metrics compared to the previous version?' Recommended method: moderated usability testing with task-based scenarios, because it combines behavioral observation (what users do) with attitudinal data (what users say), reveals specific usability issues, and provides actionable design insights. A survey alone would only capture opinions, missing the behavioral data needed to improve the design.

rubric.key: Rewritten question is specific and measurable
Appropriate method recommended with clear rationale
Explains why the original question is insufficient

questions.question: Compare contextual inquiry with traditional user interviews. What does contextual inquiry reveal that interviews cannot, and what are its limitations?
questions.answer: Contextual inquiry reveals the full environment and real behavior — workarounds, tool usage, interruptions, physical workspace, and the gap between what users say they do and what they actually do. Traditional interviews rely on users' self-reported behavior, which is filtered by memory, social desirability, and articulation ability. Limitations of contextual inquiry: it is time-intensive (1-2 hours per session plus travel), difficult to schedule in users' real environments, observer effect can change behavior, and it requires more skilled facilitation to balance observation with probing.

rubric.key: Identifies what contextual inquiry reveals that interviews miss
Mentions the say/do gap or self-reporting limitations
Lists realistic limitations of contextual inquiry

quizzes.topic: Quantitative Methods and Information Architecture Research

questions.question: Write a 3-question SUS-style survey that measures user satisfaction with a mobile banking app's bill payment feature. Explain why you chose the scale type for each question.
questions.answer: 1. 'I found the bill payment process straightforward.' (5-point Likert: Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree) — Likert scale chosen because it measures agreement with a statement, matching SUS methodology and allowing parametric analysis. 2. 'How confident are you that your bill payment was processed correctly?' (5-point scale: Not at all confident to Extremely confident) — Confidence scale chosen because it measures the user's trust in system feedback, a critical component of payment UX. 3. 'How would you rate the overall ease of paying a bill?' (7-point scale: Very Difficult to Very Easy) — Single-ease question (SEQ) chosen because it is an industry-standard post-task measure of perceived difficulty with strong validity.

rubric.key: Appropriate scale types selected for each
Rationale for each scale type is valid

questions.question: You conducted an open card sort with 20 participants and 40 content items. The dendrogram shows two items — 'Password Reset' and 'Two-Factor Authentication' — clustered together by 18 of 20 participants, but your current navigation places them in different sections. What should you do and why?
questions.answer: The 90% agreement (18/20) is a strong signal that users' mental models group these items together. You should: (1) Move both items into the same navigation section, likely under a 'Security' or 'Account Security' category. (2) Run a tree test to validate that the new placement improves findability — card sorts reveal grouping preferences but do not prove navigation success. (3) Consider cross-linking if organizational constraints require them in different sections, so users can find the item regardless of their starting point. Simply because the current IA separates them does not mean it should — the card sort data provides strong evidence that the current structure does not match users' expectations.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets the 90% clustering as strong evidence
Recommends moving items together with reasoning
Suggests tree testing to validate the change

major_assignments.title: Mixed-Method Research Study
major_assignments.description: Design and execute a mixed-method user research study combining at least one qualitative and one quantitative method. Choose a real product or service, formulate research questions, conduct the study with real participants, analyze the data, and deliver actionable findings.
objectives: Demonstrate ability to plan a rigorous research study from scratch
Execute at least two distinct research methods with real participants
Synthesize qualitative and quantitative findings into coherent insights
deliverables: Research plan document with questions, methods, and recruiting criteria
Raw data package: interview transcripts/notes, survey responses, card sort results
Analysis artifacts: affinity diagrams, statistical summaries, dendrograms
Final research report (12-15 pages) with findings and recommendations

criteria: Research questions are specific, answerable, and actionable
Method selection is appropriate for the research questions
Recruiting criteria target the right participants
Timeline and logistics are realistic
Minimum 5 qualitative participants and 30 survey respondents
Methods executed with rigor and proper technique
Data captured thoroughly with minimal researcher bias
Participant ethics respected with informed consent
Qualitative data systematically coded and themed
Quantitative data analyzed with appropriate statistics
Mixed-method triangulation reveals deeper insights
Findings go beyond surface observations to actionable insights
Report is well-organized with clear narrative arc
Findings are supported by evidence from the data
Recommendations are specific and prioritized
Presentation is compelling and tailored for stakeholders
Rigorous research plan with well-formed questions and appropriate methods
Quality execution with sufficient sample sizes and proper technique
Deep analysis synthesizing qualitative and quantitative findings
Professional report and presentation with evidence-based recommendations

weekly_quizzes.description: Biweekly quizzes on research methods, analysis techniques, and ethical considerations

method_practice_labs.description: Hands-on lab exercises practicing individual research methods with real participants

mixed_method_study.description: Comprehensive mixed-method research study with real participants and stakeholder presentation

research_critiques.description: Written critiques of published UX research studies evaluating methodology and validity

research_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio of all research deliverables with reflective annotations

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory as they involve live participant sessions. Lecture absences beyond 3 will reduce the participation component of your grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Research deliverables have hard deadlines tied to participant scheduling. Late submissions penalized 15% per day. No extensions for the final report.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be collaborative. The mixed-method study is individual work. All participant interactions must follow IRB-approved protocols.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabricating research data is the most serious form of academic dishonesty in this course. All raw data must be verifiable. Falsification results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Survey tool (Google Forms or Typeform), card sorting tool (OptimalSort), audio recording app, spreadsheet software for quantitative analysis, and presentation software.
